comments:
Summary:
This paper evaluates a simple detector — a binary classifier trained to distinguish clean from adversarial images — and reports that such a classifier attains very high accuracy (>99%) on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and SVHN for the attacks considered (FGSM/TGSM and JSMA). The authors further show that while the detector is effective and robust to a naïve second-round attack, it suffers from a generalization limitation: performance is sensitive to the attack hyperparameter (perturbation magnitude) and to the attack algorithm. The paper argues that adversarial and clean datasets are effectively different distributions and that this explains why defenses generalize poorly.

Strengths:
- The idea is simple, practical and easy to implement; the authors provide code to reproduce experiments.
- Empirical findings are clear and useful: detection is effective against the attacks used for training but does not always generalize across magnitudes or different attack algorithms.
- The paper highlights an important empirical limitation common to several defenses (binary detection, adversarial training, defensive distillation), which is valuable for the community.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Limited scope of attacks and threat models: the experiments appear limited to FGSM/TGSM and JSMA. Stronger or more modern attacks (iterative PGD, optimization-based attacks) and adaptive attackers that know the detector are not studied. This limits the conclusions about robustness.
- Experimental detail is incomplete in the provided text: architectures, training details, exact dataset sizes, and statistics (variances, ROC curves, thresholds) are largely absent or not visible here. Tables and figures are referenced but not fully displayed in the provided excerpt, which hampers reproducibility assessment.
- The claim that adversarial and clean data are “completely different datasets” is stated informally; there is no formal definition, metric, or quantitative analysis of distributional distance. The core hypothesis would be stronger with explicit measures (e.g., feature-space distances, manifold analysis) or theoretical grounding.
- No evaluation against an adaptive adversary that attempts to craft adversarials specifically to evade the binary detector; the “second-round” test described seems limited and may not represent a fully adaptive attack.
- Presentation suffers from some repetition, typographical errors and formatting issues in the provided text; clarity could be improved and the paper would benefit from better organization of experimental results.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Broaden the experimental suite to include stronger iterative and optimization-based attacks and adaptive attackers that are aware of the detector and can backpropagate through it (or use transfer attacks crafted against surrogate detectors).
- Provide more experimental details: model architectures, training hyperparameters, dataset sizes, class breakdowns, and full quantitative evaluation (ROC curves, precision/recall, false positive rates at fixed detection rates, confidence intervals).
- Quantify the claimed distributional disparity between clean and adversarial datasets (e.g., using distances in feature space, dimensionality/manifold tests, or statistical divergence measures) and analyze how this disparity varies by attack and perturbation magnitude.
- Investigate whether combining detectors with other defenses (e.g., adversarial training, input transformations) improves generalization, and whether ensembles of detectors trained on diverse attacks offer better robustness.
- Clean up presentation: remove repetition, fix typos, and ensure all tables/figures are clearly labeled and discussed.

Overall assessment:
The paper provides a useful empirical contribution: a simple detection approach can separate adversarial from clean inputs for the particular attacks and parameters used to train the detector, and the authors document a practical generalization limitation encountered by several defenses. However, the work is mainly empirical and somewhat incremental; the experiments are not yet comprehensive enough to support broad claims about adversarial datasets being fundamentally different in all settings. With more extensive experimental evaluation, clearer definitions and stronger analysis, the contribution would be more compelling.
